passages.explanation: The ascent to the locked room is staged like a Gothic ritual — shadows, rattling windows, whispered voices. The dusty, decayed room is a physical manifestation of Dorian's hidden inner life, the neglected truth behind the beautiful facade. Wilde's detail that the room 'looked as if it had not been lived in for years' is deeply ironic: this is in fact the most lived-in room in the house, the only space where Dorian's real self exists.

The unveiling of the portrait is the novel's climactic revelation, and Basil's horror at recognizing his own work — his own signature in vermilion — forces him to confront his complicity. The portrait retains traces of the beauty Basil originally captured, making the corruption all the more grotesque by contrast. Wilde explores how the artist cannot control what becomes of his creation; Basil's ideal has been transformed into something monstrous by the life of its subject.

Dorian watches Basil's reaction 'with that strange expression that one sees on the faces of those who are absorbed in a play' — he has become a spectator of his own moral catastrophe, treating real suffering as performance. The detail of smelling a flower while Basil reels in shock is quintessential Dorian: aestheticizing the moment to maintain emotional distance. Wilde shows that the ultimate corruption is not any specific sin but the inability to feel the weight of one's own actions.

The rapid-fire dialogue strips away pretense to expose raw truth. Dorian's line 'I was wrong. It has destroyed me' is perhaps his most honest moment — a flash of genuine self-knowledge immediately buried by the momentum of events. Basil's desperate rational explanations (damp, mineral poison in paints) show the mind's refusal to accept the supernatural, preferring any material explanation to a spiritual one. Wilde dramatizes the collision between rationalism and the inexplicable.

The exchange about ideals is devastating: Basil called his portrait an ideal, and Dorian forces him to see what that ideal has become. The line 'It is the face of my soul' is both confession and accusation — Dorian acknowledges his corruption while implicating Basil's worship of beauty as its origin. Basil's cry that 'It has the eyes of a devil' marks the death of his illusions, and Dorian's response — 'Each of us has heaven and hell in him' — is Wilde's most direct statement about the duality of human nature.

Basil's plea for prayer and repentance represents the Christian moral framework that Dorian has entirely abandoned. His recognition that 'I worshipped you too much' is a crucial admission: Basil's idolatry of beauty was itself a form of sin, and the portrait's corruption is as much a judgment on the worshipper as on the worshipped. Dorian's 'It is too late' is not just despair but a philosophical position — he has gone so far into aesthetic amorality that moral categories no longer apply to him.

The murder is presented as almost involuntary — hatred 'suggested to him by the image on the canvas, whispered into his ear by those grinning lips.' Wilde implies that the portrait has become an active malevolent force, or that Dorian's corruption has reached the point where it generates its own momentum. The murder of Basil is symbolically the murder of conscience, of the artist who created the mirror Dorian cannot bear to face. The brutal physicality of the stabbing contrasts shockingly with the aesthetic refinement of everything that preceded it.

The aftermath is rendered with chilling calm. The sky 'like a monstrous peacock's tail' is beautiful and grotesque simultaneously — nature itself seems to reflect the fusion of beauty and horror that defines Dorian's world. The street scene below (policeman, hansom, staggering woman) represents the ordinary life continuing oblivious to the extraordinary evil above. Dorian's refusal to look at the body and his thought that 'the secret of the whole thing was not to realize the situation' reveals his ultimate coping strategy: dissociation as a way of life.

Dorian's methodical creation of an alibi — slipping out, ringing the bell, performing surprise at the hour — shows how completely his double life has trained him in deception. The exchange with the valet about Hallward leaving at eleven to catch his train is chillingly smooth. Wilde demonstrates that a lifetime of aesthetic self-fashioning has made Dorian a consummate actor; the skills of social performance are identical to the skills of concealing murder.

The chapter's final image — Dorian searching the Blue Book for Alan Campbell's address — transforms the murder's aftermath into a practical problem to be solved. The shift from existential horror to cold logistics mirrors Dorian's own emotional shutdown. By ending on this note of calculated planning, Wilde shows that Dorian has crossed a threshold: he is no longer merely corrupt but actively predatory, treating other people as instruments to be used for self-preservation.
